sections.text: Welcome back to AP Computer Science A. Module Nine — Inheritance. This is one of the four pillars of object-oriented programming, and it's where Java starts to feel powerful. By the end of this lesson you'll know how to extend a class, override methods, and use polymorphism to write code that's flexible and reusable.

A Cat IS-AN Animal. A Dog IS-AN Animal. Both share what every Animal has — a name, an age, the ability to eat. But each also adds its own thing. Cats purr. Dogs fetch. Instead of writing Animal twice, we write it ONCE — and then Cat and Dog inherit from it. That's the whole idea of inheritance. Reuse, don't repeat.

Four areas today. One — extends and super, how a class inherits from another. Two — overriding methods, when the child wants to do things differently. Three — polymorphism, the big idea that makes inheritance powerful. And four — abstract classes, the Object class, and instanceof. Dense material. We go slow.

Here's the syntax. The keyword extends. Class Cat extends Animal means Cat is a subclass of Animal. Cat automatically gets every non-private member Animal has. We didn't redeclare name. We didn't redeclare eat. Cat just gets them. That's inheritance in one line of code.

When you write a constructor in the subclass, the first thing it must do is call the parent's constructor. That's what super of n does — it runs Animal's constructor with the name n. If you don't write super explicitly, Java tries to call super of no arguments, and if that doesn't exist, your code won't compile. Super must be the FIRST line.

What does the subclass actually inherit? Every non-private member of the superclass. Public methods, protected fields, package-private things — all yours. Private fields are NOT directly accessible — you have to go through protected fields or accessor methods. But for methods, you call them on a subclass instance as if they were defined right there.

Overriding. The subclass redefines a method that the superclass already had — same name, same parameters, same return type. Animal's sound prints generic. Cat's sound prints meow. The at-Override annotation is optional but strongly recommended — it tells the compiler to check you actually matched the parent's signature.

Your turn. Write a class Cat that extends Animal. Give it a constructor that takes a name and passes it to super. Then override the sound method to print meow. Use at-Override. Pause now, write it out, press play when you're ready.

Time. Public class Cat extends Animal. Constructor Cat of String n, body calls super of n. Then at-Override, public void sound, body prints meow. That's the full pattern. Extends, super in the constructor, at-Override on the redefined method. You'll write this shape hundreds of times in this course.

Here's the big idea. Animal a equals new Cat. The variable a is declared as Animal — the general type. But the actual object is a Cat. When we call a dot sound, Java prints meow — because the object is a Cat. The variable type decides what you can CALL. The object type decides what actually RUNS.

This is called late binding, or dynamic dispatch. Java waits until the program is actually running to decide which version of sound to call. It looks at the actual object, not the declared variable. That's the engine behind polymorphism — and it's why one line of code can behave differently for Cat, Dog, or any future Animal subclass.

Every class in Java implicitly extends one root class — Object. So every object you ever create has toString, equals, and hashCode for free. Override toString in your own class, and System dot out dot println starts printing something readable instead of that ugly at-sign hash code. It's the simplest upgrade you can make to any class you write.

An abstract class is a class you can't instantiate. New Shape would not compile. It exists to define a contract — to say, every Shape MUST have an area method. Circle and Rectangle extend Shape and are forced to implement area. Abstract classes are how you say, this is a category — but not a thing you can build directly.

Sometimes you need to check what type an object actually is at runtime. That's instanceof. If a instanceof Cat is true, then a really is a Cat — or a subclass of Cat. After the check you can safely cast — Cat c equals open paren Cat close paren a — and then call cat-specific methods like purr.

Don't mix these up. Overriding — same name, same signature, in a SUBCLASS. The child replaces the parent's version. Decided at runtime. Overloading — same name, different parameters, in the SAME class. Decided at compile time. Students confuse these constantly. Overriding is across classes. Overloading is within one class.

Recap. Extends gives you an IS-A relationship. Super calls the parent's constructor. Override redefines a parent method with the same signature. Polymorphism means the variable type is general but the object type decides what runs — dynamic dispatch. Object is the root of every class in Java. Abstract classes can't be instantiated. Instanceof tests the real type at runtime. Your assignment is in the dashboard — build a Shape hierarchy.

This video is the overview. To actually master inheritance you need to write it. Read the inheritance chapter on Runestone. Do the Codingbat AP-1 inheritance set. Submit the Shape hierarchy assignment. Book an advisor check-in if polymorphism still feels fuzzy. Next up — Module Ten, Recursion.
